translations.translation:
Xu Yi (Jicai)
徐奕 (季才)
Lived AD ?–ca.219
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
Scroll 12 (Wei 12)
byZL181
onOctober 4th, 2023

Xu Yi, styledJicai, was fromDongguan[東莞].
He evaded danger to theJiangdong region[江東], whereSun Cetreated him with respect to recruit him.[Xu] Yichanged his surname and forename, wore plain clothing, and returned to his home commandery.
When theGrand ProgenitorbecameExcellency of Works, he was recruited to serve asbureaucratic clerkand followed in a western expedition againstMa Chao. When[Ma] Chaowas defeated, the army returned. At the time, theGuanzhong Basin[關中] had recently submitted and was not yet extremely secure.[Xu] Yiremained behind to serve asChief Clerkunder theAssisting Chancellor, guarding and comforting the western capital. The western capital praised him as having prestige and credibility.
He was shifted to beInspectorofYong province[雍州刺史], then returned to serve asclerk of the Eastern Bureau. The likes ofDing Yiwere favored at the time, and though they all hindered him,[Xu] Yiwas ultimately unmoved.AHe left to becomeGrand AdministratorofWei commandery[魏郡太守].
TheBook of Weistates: Someone told[Xu] Yi, “Regarding the frankness of Shi Yu [史魚], how can it compare to the wisdom of Qu Boyu [蘧伯玉]?1Ding Yiis being favored and valued right now. You should think about how you can lower yourself to him.”[Xu] Yisaid, “TheExcellencyis wise and sagacious. How can[Ding] Yimaintain his deceit over the long term? Moreover, people who are wicked in serving their lord are the ones I can resist. Yet, you would rather that others controlme.”•••
Shi Qiu [史鰌], styled Ziyu [子魚], was a Wei [衛] official known for giving frank advice to the Duke. He recommended Qu Yuan [蘧瑗], styled Boyu [伯玉], to higher positions repeatedly, but was ignored in life. When he died, his last words were about recommending Qu Yuan. Only then did the Duke listen. Qu Yuan was a man of virtue and wisdom, regarded highly by contemporaryMaster Kong.↩
TheMaster Fustates: TheMartialAugust Emperorwas most wise. However,Cui YanandXu Yiwere incorrupt and worthy men in their era. They were both eminent in theWeicourt due to their loyalty and sincerity. And yet, whenDing Yisowed discord against them,Xu Yilost his post whileCui Yanwas executed.
When theGrand Progenitorwent on an expedition againstSun Quan, he shifted him to serve asChief Clerk Left Behind to Manage Bureaus. He told[Xu] Yi, “Your loyalty and brilliance have not been surpassed by the ancients! However, even in minor matters are you extremely strict. In the past, Xi Menbao [西門豹] wore soft leather to slow himself.2Being able to control the hardy and strong through gentleness and delicacy is what I hope from you. For now, I put you, sir, in charge of matters at home. I have no worries that would make me lookback!”•••
Xi Menbao, who was impatient by nature, wore a soft leather belt to remind himself to be more flexible.↩
When theState of Weiwas established, he served asSecretariat Minister, concurrently moderating nominations, then was promoted toDirector of the Secretariat.
When theGrand ProgenitorconqueredHanzhong[漢中],Wei Fengand others plotted to rebel. Thus,Capital CommandantYang Junwas demoted. TheGrand Progenitorsigned, “The reason why[Wei] Fengdared to nurture a rebellious mindset was because among my military ministers,3there is none who can check evil and guard against schemes. How can I get someone likeZhuge Fengwho can replace[Yang] Jun?”Huan Jiesaid, “Xu Yiis such a man.” TheGrand Progenitorthen appointed[Xu] YiasCapital Commandant, writing an order thatstated:•••
Here, I translated “claws and teeth” [爪牙] as “military ministers”.↩
“Of old, when Chu had Ziyu [子玉], theCivilDukeshifted in his mat when sitting due to him;4and when Ji An [汲黯] was in court, the [Princeof] Huainan [淮南] ended his schemes due to him.5TheBook of Odesstates, ‘It is he who oversees honesty in the country!’ Sir, this is yourdescription!”•••
Cheng Dechen [成得臣], styled Ziyu [子玉], was a general of the Chu state. Duke Chong’er of Jin had feared Cheng Dechen, even saying, “Ziyu is still alive. How could I be glad?” [子玉猶在，庸可喜乎！]↩
Ji An [汲黯], styled Changru [長孺], was a minister of theHan dynasty. The Prince of Huainan was afraid of him, saying, "[Ji] An is good at giving frank admonishments. He will defend moral integrity and die for righteousness.” [黯好直諫，守節死義] Ultimately, the Prince did not rebel.↩
He was in this post for several months, but became seriously ill and requested to resign. He was conferred to serve asGrandee Remonstrant. He thendied.B
TheBook of Weistates: When theCivil Emperorand the court ministers assembled, never did he not sigh sadly when reminiscing about what sort of person[Xu] Yiwas.[Xu] Yihad no sons, so an edict had the son of his clansman,[Xu] Tong, serve asGentlemanand receive the succession of[Xu] Yi.
